name: Robert Tanner
chief_complaint: My back went out three days ago and now I'm having trouble controlling my bladder and my legs feel numb.
onset_description: Was lifting heavy boxes at work 3 days ago when he felt a sudden pop in his lower back followed by severe pain. Pain initially was in the low back only but has progressively radiated to both legs. Yesterday developed numbness in his inner thighs and around his buttocks. This morning noticed difficulty starting his urine stream and feels like he cannot fully empty his bladder.

low_back_pain.description: It started with a pop when I was lifting a heavy box. The pain is constant and deep in my lower back. It's bad but the numbness is what really scares me.
low_back_pain.onset: 3 days ago, acute onset with lifting
low_back_pain.character: deep, constant, aching with sharp exacerbations
low_back_pain.radiation: bilateral lower extremities, posterior thighs to calves
alleviating_factors: lying flat on back with knees bent

bilateral_leg_pain.description: Both my legs are aching and burning, from my butt all the way down to my feet. The left is worse than the right.
bilateral_leg_pain.character: burning, shooting pain in sciatic distribution bilaterally
bilateral_leg_pain.location: bilateral posterior thighs, calves, and feet, left greater than right

saddle_anesthesia.description: The area between my legs and around my rear end feels numb and tingly, like it's falling asleep. It's a really strange feeling.
saddle_anesthesia.character: numbness and paresthesias in perineal and perianal distribution
saddle_anesthesia.location: perineum, perianal area, inner thighs bilaterally

urinary_retention.description: I can barely pee. I feel like I have to go really bad but when I try, it just trickles. And I've wet myself twice, which has never happened to me before.
urinary_retention.character: difficulty initiating stream, incomplete emptying, overflow incontinence

bilateral_leg_weakness.description: My legs feel heavy and weak. I'm having trouble lifting my feet when I walk. I tripped twice on the way in because my feet are dragging.
bilateral_leg_weakness.character: bilateral lower extremity weakness, worse distally, bilateral foot drop developing
bilateral_leg_weakness.location: bilateral lower extremities, worse in ankles and feet

numbness_and_tingling_legs.description: Both feet and lower legs are tingling and feel like they're going to sleep. The numbness is creeping up.
numbness_and_tingling_legs.location: bilateral feet and lower legs, ascending to mid-thigh

symptoms_absent: bowel incontinence (but reports decreased rectal sensation)

labs.CBC: WBC 8.4, Hgb 15.2, Plt 234 - within normal limits
labs.BMP: Na 141, K 4.2, Cr 1.0, BUN 16, Glucose 98 - within normal limits
labs.CRP: 8 mg/L (mildly elevated, likely from acute injury)
labs.post_void_residual: 480 mL (markedly elevated, normal <100 mL, confirms urinary retention)
labs.MRI_lumbar_spine: Large central disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the cauda equina. Moderate central canal stenosis at L5-S1. No evidence of tumor or infection.

past_medical_history: Chronic low back pain (10 years, managed conservatively)
Lumbar disc bulge L4-L5 (diagnosed on MRI 5 years ago, was small and treated conservatively)
Mild degenerative disc disease lumbar spine
surgical_history: Arthroscopic right knee surgery for meniscus tear (2010)
medications: Ibuprofen 800mg three times daily (started 3 days ago for back pain)

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 8 years ago, 20 pack-year history
social_history.occupation: Warehouse supervisor, involves heavy lifting and prolonged standing. Has been doing this for 25 years.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and 16-year-old son in a single-story home. Wife works full time as an administrative assistant.
social_history.exercise: Used to play recreational softball and go to the gym. Has been less active the past few years due to back pain.
social_history.diet: Regular diet, wife cooks most meals. Eats fast food for lunch at work.

conditions.father: Chronic back problems, had spinal surgery at age 60, hypertension, alive at 78
conditions.brother: Herniated disc, managed conservatively, healthy otherwise at 47

personality: Stoic and tough, a working man who pushes through pain. Waited too long to come in because he thought the back pain would resolve on its own. Now genuinely frightened by the neurological symptoms but tries to mask his fear. Doesn't want to seem weak.
communication_style: Matter-of-fact and brief. Gives short answers unless prompted for detail. Uses plain working-class language. Avoids eye contact when discussing embarrassing symptoms like incontinence. Needs to be asked directly about sensitive symptoms as he won't volunteer them.
pain_description_style: Understates pain due to masculine stoicism. Says 'it's bad' rather than elaborating. When asked for a number, tends to rate lower than expected. Will wince or shift position to reveal true pain level. Describes pain in practical terms ('can't work,' 'can't sit').

critical_findings: Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia + urinary retention + bilateral leg weakness = surgical emergency
Post-void residual 480 mL confirming neurogenic bladder
Progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness with developing foot drop
Large central disc herniation at L4-L5 compressing cauda equina on MRI
Progressive neurological deterioration over 3 days (delayed presentation worsens prognosis)
Decreased rectal tone on exam suggesting S2-S4 involvement
Time-critical: surgical decompression ideally within 24-48 hours of symptom onset for best outcomes
rubric.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome secondary to large central L4-L5 disc herniation
differential_diagnoses: Epidural abscess (less likely without fever or risk factors)
Severe bilateral lumbar radiculopathy without true cauda equina
recommended_interventions: EMERGENT neurosurgery or spine surgery consult - this is a surgical emergency
Urgent surgical decompression (laminectomy and discectomy) - ideally within 24-48 hours
Foley catheter insertion for urinary retention
Strict bed rest and spine precautions
IV dexamethasone 10mg to reduce edema and inflammation
Pain management with IV opioids and muscle relaxants
Serial neurological examinations (motor, sensory, reflexes, rectal tone)
DVT prophylaxis with sequential compression devices
Educate patient about surgical urgency and potential for incomplete neurological recovery given delayed presentation
Social work consult for occupational disability and rehabilitation planning
